A549-HBE-ALI comparison analysis

1. Alignment and DEA

Raw datasets: A549, primary hBECs (submersion culture, HBE), and primary hBECs (air-liquid interface culture, ALI) treated with IL1B, glucocorticoid, or both at 6h. This is actually three experiments at different timepoints, so for now we use their shared timepoint (6 h post-treatment).

Alignment performed with kallisto version 0.46.1 and normalization/DEA performed with sleuth version something on the cluster. Everything documented in kallisto-sleuth.md. I used individual sleuth runs for each cell type, modeled as ~treatment. Effect sizes (“log2fold”) and FDR are from Wald test using unstimulated (NS) samples as baseline. I also modeled everything together under model ~treatment*condition for interaction testing. Finally, raw tpm values were extracted.

2. Understanding individual variation in gene expression

2.1. Donors vs A549

Previously, we saw that only ~17% of the IL1B-regulated gene signature in A549s is shared with at least one primary celltype. See Venn diagrams below for DEGs (log2fold <= -1 or >= 1 & FDR < 0.05) in each cell type (panel A), up-DEGs (panel B), and down-DEGs (panel C). When looking for “regulation” in general, A549s look very different from primary cells. Splitting into up- and down-regulated groups reveals that most of this difference is attributable to downregulated genes. i.e., A549s are a reasonable model for IL1B-responsive gene induction but not suppression in primary cells. If we just look at upregulated genes, ~33% of the IL1B-regulated gene signature in A549s is shared with at least one primary celltype.

One possible reason for differences: A549s are derived from one individual, so four sequencing replicates may have low variability + higher significance. Each sequencing replicate for primary cells is a different individual, so higher variability + lower significance. Ignoring FDR entirely (i.e., only using the abs(log2fold) >= 1 threshold), A549 and primary cells actually have similarly sized regulated gene pools (panel A). In A549s, variation in tpm-derived log2fold values tend to be lower (panel B) and a greater proportion of the rep pool meets log2fold thresholds (panel C).

Fixed: problem 040626, discrepancy between Venn and spread gene counts.

Where does each primary donor sit in relation to A549? For each primary cell donor, check overlap of log2fold-threshold-meeting genes in that donor (top circle) vs A549 cells (bottom circle). Plots are also tagged with Jaccard index (J) and total number of genes (n, union size). This analysis was performed on regulated genes in general (panel A), IL1B-upregulated genes (panel B), and IL1B-downregulated genes (panel C)

“Fixed” colors on Venns. This package doesn’t let you specify colors for different groups, so I set everything to white instead.

Some summary metrics for the pairwise Venns:

  • Union = everything in the A549 lobe, the donor lobe, and the intersect
  • Overlap = genes expressed in both A549 and the donor
  • Jaccard = \(\frac{\text{overlap}}{\text{union}}\), high = big overlap, low = little to no overlap

Each dot is one donor-to-A549 comparison. We see that overlaps are bigger when focusing on upregulated genes, and smaller when focusing on downregulated genes. Again showing that most commonality between cell types sits in the upregulated gene fraction. T-test between \(Jaccard_{\text{upreg}}\) and \(Jaccard_{\text{downreg}}\), the bottom right panel, shows P=0.0001169.

Where do primary donors and A549s sit in relation to each other?

This is technically a 10-way comparison so Venns no longer work – use an UpSet plot instead. Panel A is the intersect between all donors and A549, B is the intersect between donors only. Notice 70 genes shared between all donors (B), where 54 of these are also shared with A549 (A).

Now split the visualization down from all regulated genes to upregulated and downregulated genes (see below). Notice how 50 of the 54 “universally shared” genes present in the upregulated fraction (panel C), and 70 of 70 “donor-shared” genes present in the upregulated fraction (panel E). However, DEGs unique to each donor still dominate over DEGs shared between donors.

2.2. IN PROGRESS: Donors vs donors

“Toy” example: at 5+ sets – Venn impossible, Euler misleading, UpSet pretty good

Starting with IL1B-upregulated genes in ALIs, I wanted to quantify how much of this signature is shared with A549s. The Euler diagram (panel A) suggested that only ~20% of genes were shared between A549s and at least one ALI donor – which was surprising. Even more surprisingly, most donors did not have connections with A549s at all…

However, when we look at the full UpSet plot (panel B) we find that another ~20% of A549-donor-shared genes exist in small intersections involving one or two donors, which the Euler diagram cannot represent. A manual sum reveals that A549s actually share 1488/2859 (52%) of IL1B-upregulated genes with at least one ALI donor.

After writing and testing R code that could recreate the manually-calculated “52%” number from above, I scaled the analysis to ask; for each donor, how much of its IL1B-regulated gene signature is shared with at least one other donor?

  1. A549s have similar # of regulated genes when compared to donors (top left plot) – aligns with previous observations

  2. This holds true for up and downregulated genes (top right plot) – ??? split the barplot from section 2.1. and see if this is correct???

  3. Given any donor, ~90% of its regulated genes are shared with at least one other donor (bottom left plot). A549s are an outlier at ~70%, but this is still pretty good. Basically means that ~70% of the transcriptome we study in A549s represents at least one of our donors. If we sequence more donors, the percentage could increase.

  4. Low sharedness by A549s is mostly attributed to downregulated genes and not upregulated genes (bottom right plot).
